conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I can give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for reaching out. I'll be happy to help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children - Alex, who is 16, and Blake, who is 5.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex (16) and Blake (5), I need to ask you a few more questions.

Let me start with your general situation: Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand more about each child's situation.

Let me ask about Blake first (who is 5): Does Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Blake doesn't live with you, I need to ask: Do you pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after Blake - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't contribute financially towards Blake at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Now let me ask about Alex (who is 16): Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Since Alex doesn't live with you either, I need to ask: Do you pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after Alex - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with Alex. I contribute towards things like clothes, school supplies, and other bits and bobs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 16, I also need to know: Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in college doing A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just to confirm the details about your contributions for Alex - you mentioned you contribute towards clothes, school supplies, and other bits and bobs. 

Can you tell me approximately how much you contribute per week or per month towards Alex's upkeep? I need to understand if your contributions are at least equal to the Child Benefit amount (which is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, or £16.95 per week for additional children).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also check: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives with their other parent who already claims Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And what about Alex - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I have one final question: Do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 per year? (This doesn't affect eligibility, but it may affect whether you need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not get into my income details right now - I'm mainly just trying to understand if I'm eligible at this point.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine - I have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Based on my assessment, here's your eligibility for Child Benefit:**

**For Alex (16 years old): You ARE eligible ✓**

Alex is 16 and in approved education (doing A-levels at college), which means Child Benefit can continue until they turn 20 as long as they remain in approved education or training. Although Alex doesn't live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child. Under the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you are eligible to claim.

**For Blake (5 years old): You are NOT eligible ✗**

Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child. Additionally, you confirmed that you don't contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep at the moment. Even if no one was claiming, you would not qualify as you don't live with Blake and aren't contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (doing A-levels at college), which means Child Benefit can continue until age 20 for children in approved education or training. Although Alex does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child. Under the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 5 years old and does not live with you. You confirmed that you do not contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep at the moment. Additionally, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and since someone else is already claiming for Blake, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. Furthermore, even if no one was claiming, you would not qualify as you are not living with Blake and are not contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (16) and Blake (5). The claimant lives in the UK. For Alex, the claimant is eligible because Alex is in approved education (A-levels), the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (exceeding the Child Benefit threshold), and no one else is claiming for Alex. For Blake, the claimant is not eligible because Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim per child. Additionally, the claimant does not contribute financially to Blake's upkeep.
